Chapter 6: Chemical Equilibrium Chemical Equilibrium in the Environment
North Branch of the Potomac River runs through the Appalachian Mountains
Potomac River
Acid drainage from abandoned coal mines lowers the pH of the river to 4.5.
Papermill downstream releases calcium carbonate.
Bacterial respiration at a wastewater treatment plan adds CO 2 to the system.
Systems combine to restore river pH.
Coral Reefs
Same chemistry that helps Potomac River endangers the coral reefs.
Rising CO 2 levels in oceans are lowering the pH.
Low pH dissolves CaCO 3 from coral.

Common types of reactions encountered in chemical analysis
Precipitation – takes place when the combination of two or more soluble chemicals produces an insoluble substance or precipitate .
Complex formation – involves formation of a reversible complex between two or more chemicals, such as a reaction based on a coordinate covalent bond or non covalent interactions.
Acid-base reaction – transfer of hydrogen ion from one compound (an acid) to another (base).
• Oxidation-reduction reaction –an exchange of electrons between chemicals.
• Chemical processes that involve a change in the environment of a chemical.
Quantitative Chemical Analysis, Daniel C. Harris and Charles A. Lucy, © 2020 W. H. Freeman and Company

Describing Chemical Reactions
Questions asked about chemical process:
- “What amount of a chemical is required in the reaction or process”.  “How much of this chemical is actually present”.
- “ How does the ability of a chemical to `react change as we alter its 	surroundings” – Chemical activity .
- “How much energy will be given off or required by this process? or  How far might the chemical process proceed?– Chemical thermodynamics .
- “How fast does a chemical process occur” - Kinetics .
Quantitative Chemical Analysis, Daniel C. Harris and Charles A. Lucy, © 2020 W. H. Freeman and Company

Chemical equilibrium
Chemical equilibrium is a dynamic state in which the rates of the forward and the reverse reactions are identical.
• Chemical reactions do not cease at equilibrium, instead, the amounts of reactants and products are constant.
 Equilibrium constant
* predict the direction and completeness of a chemical reaction.
* yields no information concerning the rate at which equilibrium is approached.

The Equilibrium Constant
a A + b B
c C + d D
Equilibrium constant, K
Reaction is favored if K > 1.
Equilibrium constants are dimensionless.
If A is a solute, [A] means [A]/(1 M).
If D is a gas, [D] means (pressure of D in bars)/(1 bar) – written as P D .
Each quantity in the ratio is given as concentration at standard state .
Concentration of solutes expressed as mol/L
Concentration of gases expressed in bars
Concentration of pure solids, pure liquids, and solvents omitted because they are unity

Manipulating Equilibrium Constants
If the direction of a reaction is reversed, the new value of K is simply the reciprocal of the original value of K .
If two reactions are added, the new K is the product of the two individual equilibrium constants.
If n reactions are added, the overall equilibrium constant is the product of n individual equilibrium constants.

The equilibrium constant for the reaction H 2 O
H + + OH − is called K W (= [H + ][OH − ]) and has the value 1.0 × 10 −14 at 25°C. Given that

Equilibrium and Thermodynamics
Enthalpy
The heat absorbed or released (enthalpy) and the dispersal of energy into molecular motions (entropy) independently contribute to the equilibrium constant.
Enthalpy ( H ): The enthalpy change, Δ H , for a reaction is the heat absorbed or released when the reaction takes place under constant applied pressure.
If Δ H is positive, heat is absorbed and the reaction is endothermic .
If Δ H is negative, heat is released and the reaction is exothermic .
The standard enthalpy change , Δ H °, refers to the heat absorbed or released when the reactants and products are in their standard states.

Entropy
Entropy ( S ): When a chemical or physical change occurs in a reversible manner at a constant temperature, the entropy change, Δ S , is equal to the heat absorbed ( q rev ) divided by the temperature ( T ).
A gas has higher entropy than a liquid, and a liquid has higher entropy than a solid.
Δ S ° is the change in entropy when all species are in their standard state.
If Δ S is positive, the products have greater entropy than the reactants.
If Δ S is negative, the products have lower entropy than the reactants.

Free Energy
The change in Gibbs free energy ( Δ G ) is the arbiter between opposing tendencies of Δ H and Δ S . At constant temperature ( T ):
A reaction is favored if Δ G is negative.
Free energy relates the equilibrium constant to energetics of a reaction.
Δ G ° = (+) K < 1
Reaction is disfavored
Δ G ° = (−) K > 1
Reaction is favored
Satisfy yourself that K > 1 if Δ G ° is negative.

Le Châtelier’s Principle (1 of 2)
If a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change or disturbance:
The direction in which the system proceeds back to equilibrium will attempt to offset the change.
If the concentration of Cr 3+ is increased, a stress is placed on the system.
The reaction quotient , Q < K
So the system shifts to form more products until Q = K

Le Châtelier’s Principle (2 of 2)
If a reaction is at equilibrium and products are added (or reactants removed), the reaction goes to the left.
If a reaction is at equilibrium and reactants are added (or products removed), the reaction goes to the right.
When the temperature of a system is changed, so is the equilibrium constant.
The equilibrium constant of an endothermic reaction ( Δ H ° = +) increases if the temperature is raised.
Heat + reactants
products
The equilibrium constant of an exothermic reaction ( Δ H ° = −) decreases if the temperature is raised.
Reactants
products + heat

Equilibrium Problems
Equilibrium problems make thermodynamic predictions, not kinetic predictions.
Calculate what must happen for a system to reach equilibrium, not how long it will take.
Some reactions are over in an instant; others take a million years.
The size of an equilibrium constant tells us nothing about the rate (kinetics) of the reaction.
A large equilibrium constant does not imply that a reaction is fast.

Solubility Product ( K sp ) (1 of 2)
Solubility product ( K sp ): equilibrium constant for the reaction in which a solid salt dissolves to give its constituent ions in solution
Solids omitted from K sp expression
Numeric values found in Appendix F
Saturated solution: equilibrium with excess, undissolved solid
If aqueous solution left in contact with excess solid Hg 2 Cl 2 , solid will dissolve until the condition
is satisfied.
Solution contains all solid capable of being dissolved (under given conditions)

Solubility Product ( K sp ) (2 of 2)
Use the solubility product to find concentration of one ion when concentration of the other is known or fixed by some means.
What is the concentration of
in equilibrium with 0.10 M KCl and excess, undissolved Hg 2 Cl 2 ( s )?
Because Hg 2 Cl 2 is so slightly soluble, additional Cl − from the Hg 2 Cl 2 is negligible compared to 0.10 M Cl − .

Mercurous Ion,
Mercurous ion,
is a dimer which consists of two identical units bound together.
Disproportionation is the process in which an element in an intermediate oxidation state, such as Hg(I), gives products in both higher and lower oxidation states
OH − , S 2− , and CN − stabilize Hg(II), thereby converting Hg(I) into Hg(II) and Hg(0).

Common Ion Effect
The common ion effect is the application of Le Châtelier’s principle.
A salt is less soluble if one of its constituent ions is already present in solution.
Figure 6-1

Demonstration 6-1 Common Ion Effect
Fill two test tubes about one-third full with saturated aqueous KCl .
Add 6 M HCl to one test tube and 12 M HCl to the other.

Determining Whether Precipitation Will Occur
 If ion product (Q) is larger than K sp , a ppt will form until the concentrations of the ions have reached the point where the ion Q = K sp .
* Q is less or equal to K sp no precipitation expected to occur.

Q does not exceed the solubility product, Q < K sp for PbI 2 .
Pb 2+ will not precipitate.
Yes, the separation based on precipitation is feasible.

Izaak M. Kolthoff  (1894–1993)
Popularized a maxim from his PhD advisor, N Schoorl :
“Theory guides; experiment decides.”
Our calculation says that a separation experiment is worth trying.
Only an experiment can show whether the separation actually works.

Complex Ion Formation
Anions (X − ) that precipitate metals (M + ) are often observed to form complex ions such as
(two or more simple ions bonded to another)
A ligand is any atom or group of atoms attached to species of interest.
In complex ions such as
and
, I − is the ligand of Pb 2+ .
Pb 2+ acts as a Lewis acid and I − acts as a Lewis base .

Effect of Complex Ion Formation on Solubility
Figure 6-3
If anion X  precipitates metal M + , it is often observed that a high concentration of X  causes solid MX to redissolve .
The increased solubility arises from formation of complex ions, such as
All equilibria are satisfied simultaneously.
The concentration of M n + that satisfies one equilibrium must satisfy all equilibria. There can only be one concentration of M n + in the solution.

If Pb 2+ and I − only reacted to form solid PbI 2 , then the solubility of Pb 2+ would always be very low in the presence of excess I − .
However, high concentrations of I − cause PbI 2 ( s ) to dissolve.
This is explained by the formation of a series of complex ions:
Stepwise formation constants, K i , are defined as the addition of ONE ligand to the metal ion.
Cumulative formation constants,  i , can include MULTIPLE ligand additions to the metal ion.

The total concentration of dissolved lead is
